g.data-narration: The OS Interface bridges humans and computers, letting users manage resources and run applications efficiently.
g.text: The OS Interface forms the critical bridge between human users and computer hardware.
It provides tools to issue commands, manage resources, and execute applications.
Without this interface, direct hardware manipulation would be required for every task.

g.data-narration: Two primary interface types exist: CLI uses text commands, while GUI uses visual elements like windows and icons.
CLI follows a specific structure: user@host:~$ command [options] [arguments]. The shell interprets and executes.
Common CLI examples include 'ls -l' for listing files in Linux and 'dir C:' in Windows Command Prompt.
GUI uses the WIMP paradigm: Windows, Icons, Menus, and Pointers for visual interaction.
GUI workflow flows through layers: User click → Window Manager → OS Kernel → Hardware Interaction.
System calls allow user programs to request kernel services like process control, file management, and device communication.
g.text: System Calls: The Bridge to Kernel
System calls provide a programming interface between user applications and the OS kernel.
When a program needs hardware access, it triggers a system call to switch from user mode to kernel mode.
This ensures security and prevents unauthorized resource access.

g.data-narration: Four main system call categories exist: Process Control, File Management, Device Management, and Information Maintenance.
A C program using write() system call demonstrates how user programs directly interface with the kernel.
g.text: System Call Example: write() in C

g.data-narration: APIs are standardized library functions that wrap system calls, providing a consistent programming interface.
g.text: APIs are sets of routines and protocols for building software applications.
They provide a standardized way for programs to interact with the OS through system calls.
APIs are language-specific wrappers around OS-level system calls.

g.data-narration: APIs are language-specific wrappers, while system calls are OS-level requests. Python's os.mkdir() uses POSIX mkdir() internally.
g.text: API vs System Call: Understanding the Difference
Key Difference: API is a wrapper; System Call is the actual OS request

g.data-narration: Compare CLI and GUI across five features: learning curve, resource usage, automation, precision, and use cases.
Key takeaways: CLI offers precision and automation; GUI provides user-friendly visual interaction; system calls bridge user programs to kernel.
For exams: Remember 3 CLI vs GUI differences, 4 system call categories, and the API vs system call relationship.
g.text: ✓ Memorize CLI vs GUI differences (resource usage, automation, precision)
✓ Remember 4 system call categories: Process, File, Device, Information
✓ API = language wrapper; System Call = OS-level request
✓ Know system call flow: User Mode → Trap → Kernel Mode → Return
